battles.loc: Sekigahara, Mino Province (modern Gifu Prefecture)
battles.o: Decisive Eastern Army victory. Tokugawa Ieyasu's path to the shogunate was secured.

battles.loc: Gifu Castle, Mino Province (modern Gifu)
battles.a: Eastern Army — Ikeda Terumasa & Fukushima Masanori
battles.o: Eastern Army victory; Gifu Castle fell, removing a key Western stronghold.

battles.loc: Mimi River, Hyūga Province (modern Miyazaki Prefecture)
battles.b: Ōtomo clan — Ōtomo Sōrin (with Ōtomo Yoshimune)
battles.o: Decisive Shimazu victory; the Ōtomo invasion of Hyūga collapsed.

battles.loc: Kawanakajima plain, Shinano Province (modern Nagano)
battles.o: Tactically inconclusive across five engagements; neither clan secured the plain.

battles.loc: Azukizaka, Mikawa Province (modern Aichi Prefecture)
battles.b: Imagawa clan — Imagawa Yoshimoto (with Imagawa Sessai)
battles.o: Oda victory; Nobuhide checked Imagawa expansion into Mikawa.

battles.loc: Uedahara, Shinano Province (modern Ueda, Nagano)
battles.o: Murakami victory (pyrrhic); the first defeat of Takeda Shingen's career.

battles.loc: Suriagehara, Mutsu Province (modern Inawashiro / Bandai, Fukushima)
battles.o: Decisive Date victory; the Ashina clan was destroyed.

battles.loc: Hetsugi River (modern Ōno River), Bungo Province (modern Ōita)
battles.b: Toyotomi vanguard (Ōtomo allied) — Chōsokabe Motochika & Sengoku Hidehisa
battles.o: Shimazu victory; the Toyotomi vanguard was mauled.

battles.loc: Hitotori Bridge, Mutsu Province (modern Motomiya, Fukushima)
battles.b: Anti-Date coalition — Satake Yoshishige & allied clans
battles.o: Strategic Date survival; the coalition dissolved and withdrew.

battles.loc: Tainei-ji temple, Nagato Province (modern Nagato, Yamaguchi)
battles.o: Rebel victory; Ōuchi Yoshitaka was forced to commit seppuku.

battles.loc: Arita Castle, Aki Province (modern Hiroshima Prefecture)
battles.o: Mōri victory; Takeda Motoshige killed.

battles.loc: Mimase Pass, Sagami Province (modern Kanagawa Prefecture)
battles.a: Takeda clan — Takeda Shingen (Yamagata Masakage, Baba Nobuharu)
battles.b: Hōjō clan — Hōjō Ujiteru & Hōjō Ujikuni
battles.o: Takeda victory; the Hōjō pursuit was beaten off.

battles.loc: Tatarahama, Hakata Bay, Chikuzen Province (modern Fukuoka)
battles.b: Mōri clan — Mōri Motonari (Kikkawa Motoharu, Kobayakawa Takakage)
battles.o: Ōtomo advantage; the Mōri ultimately abandoned their Kyūshū foothold.

battles.loc: Odaihara, Saku, Shinano Province (modern Nagano) — coordinates approximate
battles.a: Takeda clan — Takeda Shingen (Itagaki Nobukata, Sanada Yukitaka)
battles.b: Yamanouchi-Uesugi clan — Kanai Hidekage (sent by Uesugi Norimasa)
battles.o: Takeda victory; the Uesugi relief of Shinano failed.

battles.loc: Sezawa, Shinano Province (modern Fujimi, Nagano)
battles.b: Shinano coalition — Suwa Yorishige, Ogasawara Nagatoki, Murakami Yoshikiyo
battles.o: Takeda victory (recorded in Kōyō Gunkan; historicity disputed).

battles.loc: Kōnodai, Shimōsa Province (modern Ichikawa, Chiba)
battles.b: Satomi–Ashikaga alliance — Satomi Yoshitaka & Ashikaga Yoshiaki (Oyumi)
battles.o: Hōjō victory; Ashikaga Yoshiaki of Oyumi killed.

battles.loc: Kōnodai, Shimōsa Province (modern Ichikawa, Chiba)
battles.a: Later Hōjō clan — Hōjō Ujiyasu (with Ujimasa & Ujiteru)
battles.b: Satomi clan — Satomi Yoshihiro (with Ōta Sukemasa)
battles.o: Decisive Hōjō victory.

battles.loc: Un no Kuchi, Shinano Province (modern Nagano Prefecture)
battles.a: Takeda clan — Takeda Nobutora & Takeda Harunobu (Shingen)
battles.o: Takeda victory; Hiraga Genshin killed.

battles.loc: Kaga Province (Takao Castle, near modern Kanazawa, Ishikawa)
battles.o: Ikkō-ikki victory; Togashi Masachika forced to suicide.

battles.loc: Sakainehara, Shimōsa Province (modern Kashiwa, Chiba)
battles.a: Ōgigayatsu-Uesugi side — Ōta Dōkan (with Chiba Yoritane)
battles.o: Uesugi/Ōta victory.

battles.loc: Iidagawara, Kai Province (modern Kōfu, Yamanashi)
battles.o: Takeda victory.

battles.loc: Ningbo, China — NOT in Japan; a clash between Japanese clan missions on Ming soil
battles.b: Hosokawa clan mission — Rankō Zuisa & Song Suqing
battles.o: Ōuchi rampage; official Ming–Japan tally trade was broken off.

battles.loc: Nashinokidaira, Kai Province (modern Yamanashi Prefecture) — coordinates approximate
battles.o: Takeda victory.

battles.loc: Ozawahara, Musashi Province (modern Asao, Kawasaki, Kanagawa)
battles.o: Hōjō victory — the first battle of the sixteen-year-old Ujiyasu.

battles.loc: Idano, Mikawa Province (modern Aichi Prefecture)
battles.a: Matsudaira clan — Matsudaira Hirotada (with Nobutaka & Yasutaka)
battles.o: Matsudaira victory; Oda Nobuhide's opportunistic invasion of Mikawa was repulsed.

battles.loc: Iwaya (Momotsugi) Castle, Satsuma Province (modern Kagoshima) — coordinates approximate
battles.a: Iriki-in clan (for Shimazu Takahisa) — Iriki-in Shigetomo
battles.o: Iriki-in victory; Iwaya Castle taken in a night raid.

battles.loc: Ankokuji, Shinano Province (modern Chino, Nagano)
battles.o: Takeda victory; Takatō Yoritsugu was defeated and his brother Yorimune was killed.

battles.loc: Kanō, Mino Province (modern Gifu) — coordinates approximate
battles.o: Saitō victory; the Oda were thrown back with heavy losses.

battles.loc: Oshikibata, Aki Province (modern Hiroshima Prefecture)
battles.b: Sue clan — Miyagawa Fusanaga (for Sue Harukata)
battles.o: Mōri victory.

battles.loc: Norada, near Hikone, Ōmi Province (modern Shiga Prefecture)
battles.o: Azai victory against more than two-to-one odds.

battles.loc: Kyōkōji, Kawachi Province (modern Yao, Osaka)
battles.o: Miyoshi victory.

battles.loc: Kizaki plain, Hyūga–Ōsumi border (modern Miyazaki Prefecture)
battles.o: Stunning Shimazu victory against ten-to-one odds.

battles.loc: Tone River, Kōzuke Province (modern Gunma Prefecture)
battles.o: Inconclusive; the rivals disengaged across the river.

battles.loc: Mimaomote, Iyo Province (modern Ehime Prefecture) — coordinates approximate
battles.b: Chōsokabe clan — Kumu Chikanobu (久武親信)
battles.o: Kōno victory; the Chōsokabe commander Kumu Chikanobu was killed.

battles.loc: Iga Province (modern Iga, Mie Prefecture) — coordinates approximate
battles.a: Oda clan — Oda Nobunaga & Oda Nobukatsu
battles.b: Iga ikki (warrior leagues) — Iga local confederation
battles.o: Oda victory; Iga was conquered and devastated.

battles.loc: Nagasaki, Hizen Province (modern Nagasaki Prefecture)
battles.b: Ryūzōji and local rivals — Ryūzōji Takanobu
battles.o: Ōmura held Nagasaki but, hard-pressed, ceded it to the Jesuits in 1580.

battles.loc: Numajiri, Shimotsuke Province (modern Tochigi) — coordinates approximate
battles.o: Satake coalition holds; a 110-day standoff ended in a negotiated peace.

battles.loc: Kanna River, Kōzuke–Musashi border (modern Gunma / Saitama)
battles.a: Later Hōjō clan — Hōjō Ujinao & Ujikuni
battles.o: Decisive Hōjō victory.

battles.loc: Nakatomi River, Awa Province (modern Tokushima, Shikoku)
battles.o: Chōsokabe victory.

battles.loc: Sendai River, Satsuma Province (north of Kagoshima, Kyūshū)
battles.o: Toyotomi victory; the Shimazu withdrew and Satsuma soon submitted.

battles.loc: Adachi & Asaka districts, Mutsu Province (modern Kōriyama, Fukushima)
battles.b: Ashina–Sōma–Satake coalition — Ashina Yoshihiro & Satake Yoshishige
battles.o: Effective Date victory; the confrontation was settled in July 1588.

battles.loc: Asai Nawate, Kaga Province (modern Komatsu, Ishikawa)
battles.o: Niwa tactical victory by ambush, though the Maeda withdrew in good order.

battles.loc: Kuise River, near Ōgaki Castle, Mino Province (modern Gifu)
battles.b: Eastern Army — Honda Tadakatsu and others
battles.o: Western Army victory; the Eastern vanguard fell back to Sekigahara.

battles.loc: Sendanno, Etchū Province (modern Toyama Prefecture) — coordinates approximate
battles.o: Ikkō-ikki victory; Nagao Tamekage was defeated and retired.

battles.loc: Shiojiri Pass, Shinano Province (modern Nagano Prefecture)
battles.o: Takeda victory by surprise dawn attack.

battles.loc: Tatarahama, Hakata Bay, Chikuzen Province (modern Fukuoka)
battles.o: Ashikaga victory; Kyūshū secured for Takauji.

battles.loc: Minato River, Settsu Province (modern Kobe, Hyōgo)
battles.b: Imperial loyalists (Southern) — Kusunoki Masashige & Nitta Yoshisada
battles.o: Decisive Ashikaga victory; Kusunoki Masashige died on the field.

battles.loc: Kanegasaki Castle, Tsuruga, Echizen Province (modern Fukui)
battles.o: Ashikaga victory; the castle fell and two imperial princes perished.

battles.loc: Ishizu, Sakai, Izumi Province (modern Sakai, Osaka)
battles.a: Ashikaga (Northern Court) — Kō no Moronao & Moroyasu
battles.o: Northern Court victory; Kitabatake Akiie was killed.

battles.loc: Shijōnawate, Kawachi Province (modern Shijōnawate, Osaka)
battles.a: Northern Court (Ashikaga) — Kō no Moronao & Moroyasu
battles.o: Northern Court victory; Kusunoki Masatsura died and Yoshino was sacked.

battles.loc: Satta Pass, Suruga Province — a key battle of the Kannō Incident (modern Shizuoka)
battles.a: Ashikaga Takauji's faction — Ashikaga Takauji (with Kō no Moronao)
battles.b: Ashikaga Tadayoshi's faction — Ashikaga Tadayoshi (allied to the Southern Court)
battles.o: Takauji prevailed; his brother Tadayoshi died in 1352.

battles.loc: Uchidehama, Settsu Province (modern Ashiya, Hyōgo)
battles.b: Ashikaga Takauji's faction — Ashikaga Takauji (with Kō no Moronao)
battles.o: Tadayoshi's faction won; Takauji's army was routed.

battles.loc: Chikugo River, near Kurume, Chikugo Province (modern Fukuoka)
battles.a: Southern Court (Seisei-fu) — Prince Kanenaga & Kikuchi Takemitsu
battles.o: Southern Court victory; Northern power in Kyūshū was broken for years.

battles.loc: Sakai, Izumi Province (modern Sakai, Osaka)
battles.o: Shogunate victory; Ōuchi Yoshihiro died and Sakai fell.

battles.loc: Kamakura, Sagami Province (modern Kanagawa) — coordinates approximate
battles.o: Mochiuji's side won; Zenshū took his own life at Kamakura.

battles.loc: Kamakura, Sagami Province (modern Kanagawa) — coordinates approximate
battles.a: Shogunate & Kantō kanrei — Ashikaga Yoshinori & Uesugi Norizane
battles.o: Shogunate victory; Mochiuji was forced to commit suicide in 1439.

battles.loc: Yūki Castle, Shimōsa Province (modern Yūki, Ibaraki)
battles.b: Yūki clan & Mochiuji loyalists — Yūki Ujitomo
battles.o: Rebellion crushed; Yūki Castle fell and the clan was destroyed.

battles.loc: Kyoto (Ashikaga shogunate) — coordinates approximate
battles.o: The shōgun was assassinated; the Akamatsu were then hunted down and destroyed.

battles.loc: Itsukushima (Miyajima), Aki Province (modern Hiroshima)
battles.o: Decisive Mōri victory; Sue Harukata committed suicide.

battles.loc: Dengaku-hazama, Owari Province (modern Toyoake, Aichi)
battles.o: Stunning Oda victory; Imagawa Yoshimoto was killed.

battles.loc: Ane River, near Nagahama, Ōmi Province (modern Shiga)
battles.a: Oda–Tokugawa alliance — Oda Nobunaga & Tokugawa Ieyasu
battles.b: Azai–Asakura alliance — Azai Nagamasa & Asakura Yoshikage
battles.o: Oda–Tokugawa victory.

battles.loc: Mikatagahara plateau, Tōtōmi Province (modern Hamamatsu, Shizuoka)
battles.o: Decisive Takeda victory; Ieyasu barely escaped to Hamamatsu.

battles.loc: Shitaragahara, near Nagashino Castle, Mikawa Province (modern Shinshiro, Aichi)
battles.a: Oda–Tokugawa alliance — Oda Nobunaga & Tokugawa Ieyasu
battles.o: Decisive Oda–Tokugawa victory; the Takeda cavalry was broken.

battles.loc: Tedori River, Kaga Province (modern Ishikawa)
battles.o: Uesugi victory; the Oda army was driven back across the river.

battles.loc: Honnō-ji temple, Kyoto
battles.o: Nobunaga was forced to take his own life; the would-be unifier was dead.

battles.loc: Yamazaki, below Tennōzan (modern Ōyamazaki, Kyoto)
battles.o: Hideyoshi's victory; Akechi Mitsuhide was defeated and killed in flight.

battles.loc: Shizugatake, near Lake Yogo, Ōmi Province (modern Nagahama, Shiga)
battles.o: Decisive Hideyoshi victory; Shibata Katsuie killed himself at Kitanoshō.

battles.loc: Komaki & Nagakute, Owari Province (modern Aichi)
battles.a: Tokugawa–Oda alliance — Tokugawa Ieyasu & Oda Nobukatsu
battles.o: Tactically a Tokugawa success (Nagakute); strategically inconclusive — settled by negotiation.

battles.loc: Okita-Nawate, Hizen Province (modern Shimabara, Nagasaki)
battles.o: Decisive Shimazu–Arima victory; Ryūzōji Takanobu was killed in action.

battles.loc: Odawara Castle, Sagami Province (modern Odawara, Kanagawa)
battles.b: Later Hōjō clan — Hōjō Ujimasa & Ujinao
battles.o: Toyotomi victory; the Hōjō surrendered and the clan was dissolved.

battles.loc: Osaka Castle, Settsu Province (modern Osaka)
battles.o: Tokugawa victory; the Toyotomi were destroyed and Osaka Castle fell.

battles.loc: Uji River / Byōdō-in, Yamashiro Province (modern Uji, Kyoto)
battles.a: Minamoto / Prince Mochihito's forces — Minamoto no Yorimasa (with Prince Mochihito)
battles.o: Taira victory; Minamoto no Yorimasa took his own life and Prince Mochihito was killed.

battles.loc: Ishibashiyama, near Odawara, Sagami Province (modern Kanagawa) — coordinates approximate
battles.o: Taira (Ōba) victory; Yoritomo was defeated and fled by sea to Awa.

battles.loc: Fuji River, Suruga Province (modern Fuji, Shizuoka) — coordinates approximate
battles.a: Minamoto clan — Minamoto no Yoritomo (with Takeda Nobuyoshi)
battles.o: Minamoto victory — essentially without a fight, as the Taira army fled.

battles.loc: Kurikara Pass (Tonamiyama), Etchū–Kaga border (modern Toyama / Ishikawa) — coordinates approximate
battles.o: Decisive Minamoto (Yoshinaka) victory.

battles.loc: Awazu, Ōtsu, Ōmi Province (modern Ōtsu, Shiga) — coordinates approximate
battles.a: Kamakura Minamoto — Minamoto no Noriyori & Ichijō Tadayori
battles.o: Yoritomo's forces victorious; Kiso Yoshinaka was killed.

battles.loc: Ichi-no-Tani, Suma, Settsu Province (modern Suma-ku, Kobe)
battles.a: Minamoto clan — Minamoto no Yoshitsune & Noriyori
battles.o: Minamoto victory; the Taira fortress fell and they fled to their ships.

battles.loc: Yashima, Sanuki Province (modern Takamatsu, Kagawa)
battles.o: Minamoto victory; the Taira abandoned Yashima and fled west by sea.

battles.loc: Dan-no-ura, Shimonoseki Strait, Nagato Province (modern Shimonoseki, Yamaguchi)
battles.b: Taira clan — Taira no Munemori (with Taira no Tomomori)
battles.o: Decisive Minamoto victory; the Taira clan was destroyed and the child-emperor Antoku drowned.

battles.loc: Uji–Seta river crossings, approaches to Kyoto (modern Kyoto) — coordinates approximate
battles.a: Kamakura shogunate — Hōjō Yasutoki (for regent Hōjō Yoshitoki)
battles.b: Retired Emperor Go-Toba's forces — Go-Toba (retired emperor)
battles.o: Decisive shogunate victory; Go-Toba was exiled to the Oki Islands.

battles.loc: Hakata Bay, Chikuzen Province (modern Fukuoka) — coordinates approximate
battles.a: Kamakura shogunate (Kyūshū defenders) — Hōjō regency (regent Hōjō Tokimune)
battles.b: Yuan (Mongol) & Goryeo invasion force — Hol Don (Hindu) & Kim Bang-gyeong
battles.o: The invaders withdrew to their ships and were scattered by a storm; Kyūshū held.

battles.loc: Hakata Bay & the defensive stone wall, Chikuzen Province (modern Fukuoka) — coordinates approximate
battles.a: Kamakura shogunate (Kyūshū defenders) — Hōjō regency (regent Hōjō Tokimune)
battles.b: Yuan (Mongol), Goryeo & Southern Song force — Eastern Route & Jiangnan armies
battles.o: Japanese victory; the vast invasion fleet was destroyed by a typhoon — the 'kamikaze'.

battles.loc: Chihaya Castle, Mt. Kongō, Kawachi Province (modern Chihayaakasaka, Osaka)
battles.o: Loyalist success; Kusunoki held out and tied down the shogunate's forces.

battles.loc: Kamakura, Sagami Province (modern Kanagawa)
battles.b: Kamakura shogunate — Hōjō regency (regent Hōjō Takatoki)
battles.o: Kamakura fell; Hōjō Takatoki and his kin took their own lives — ending the Kamakura shogunate.

battles.loc: Mouth of the Kizu River, Osaka Bay
battles.o: Initial Mōri victory (1576); Decisive Oda victory (1578).

battles.loc: Hara Castle, Shimabara Peninsula, Hizen Province (modern Nagasaki Prefecture)
battles.a: Tokugawa shogunate — Matsudaira Nobutsuna (after Itakura Shigemasa fell)
battles.o: Shogunate victory; the rebels were annihilated and Christianity driven underground.

battles.loc: Toba and Fushimi, south of Kyoto
battles.o: Decisive Imperial victory; the opening battle of the Boshin War.

battles.loc: Ueno (Kan'ei-ji), Edo / Tokyo
battles.b: Shōgitai (pro-Tokugawa retainers) — Shibusawa Seiichirō & Amano Hachirō
battles.o: Imperial victory in a single day; the Shōgitai were destroyed.

battles.loc: Tsuruga Castle, Aizuwakamatsu, Mutsu Province (modern Fukushima Prefecture)
battles.a: Imperial (Satsuma–Chōshū–Tosa) forces — Itagaki Taisuke & Yamagata Aritomo
battles.o: Imperial victory after a month-long siege; the Aizu domain was crushed.

battles.loc: Goryōkaku fortress, Hakodate, Ezo (modern Hokkaido)
battles.b: Ezo Republic (Tokugawa loyalists) — Enomoto Takeaki & Hijikata Toshizō
battles.o: Imperial victory; the surrender at Goryōkaku ended the Boshin War.

battles.loc: Kyoto (Kamigyō / Nishijin), Yamashiro Province
battles.o: Inconclusive stalemate; Kyoto was devastated and shogunal authority collapsed, opening the Sengoku period.

battles.loc: Kagoshima Bay, Satsuma Province (modern Kagoshima)
battles.b: British Royal Navy — Vice-Admiral Augustus Kuper
battles.o: Inconclusive bombardment — but it pushed Satsuma toward Britain and rapid modernization.

battles.loc: Shimonoseki Strait, Nagato Province (modern Yamaguchi)
battles.b: Allied fleet (Britain, France, Netherlands, USA) — Admiral Augustus Kuper
battles.o: Decisive Allied victory; Chōshū's batteries were destroyed and the strait reopened.

battles.loc: Hamaguri Gate, Kyoto Imperial Palace
battles.a: Shogunate forces (Aizu & Satsuma) — Matsudaira Katamori & Saigō Takamori
battles.b: Chōshū Domain — Kusaka Genzui & Kijima Matabei
battles.o: Shogunate victory; Chōshū was driven from Kyoto and branded an 'enemy of the court.'

battles.loc: The Chōshū borders (Suō / Nagato), four fronts around Yamaguchi
battles.a: Chōshū Domain — Mōri Takachika; cmdrs. Takasugi Shinsaku & Ōmura Masujirō
battles.b: Tokugawa shogunate coalition — Shōgun Tokugawa Iemochi
battles.o: Chōshū victory — the shogunate's punitive campaign collapsed, exposing its weakness.

battles.loc: Sashima, Shimōsa Province (modern Bandō, Ibaraki)
battles.a: Imperial court forces — Taira no Sadamori & Fujiwara no Hidesato
battles.o: Masakado defeated and killed (940), but the revolt exposed the court's weakness.

battles.loc: Iyo Province & the Seto Inland Sea (modern Ehime)
battles.a: Imperial court forces — Ono no Yoshifuru & Minamoto no Tsunemoto
battles.o: Suppressed in 941; Sumitomo killed. Paired with Masakado, it shook the court east and west.

battles.loc: Mutsu Province — the Kitakami valley (modern Iwate)
battles.b: Abe clan — Abe no Yoritoki & Abe no Sadato
battles.o: Minamoto victory (1063); the Abe destroyed and the Minamoto rooted in the east.

battles.loc: Dewa Province — the Kanezawa stockade (modern Yokote, Akita)
battles.a: Minamoto & Fujiwara no Kiyohira — Minamoto no Yoshiie
battles.b: Kiyowara clan — Kiyowara no Iehira & Takehira
battles.o: Yoshiie wins, but the court refuses to call it an official war — so he pays his men himself.

battles.loc: Kyoto — the Shirakawa-den palace
battles.a: Emperor Go-Shirakawa's faction — Taira no Kiyomori & Minamoto no Yoshitomo
battles.b: Retired Emperor Sutoku's faction — Minamoto no Tameyoshi & Taira no Tadamasa
battles.o: Go-Shirakawa's side wins; Sutoku is exiled — and the samurai become the arbiters of the court.

battles.loc: Kyoto — the Sanjō Palace
battles.b: Minamoto & Fujiwara no Nobuyori — Minamoto no Yoshitomo
battles.o: Decisive Taira victory; Yoshitomo is killed and his young son Yoritomo exiled — setting up the Genpei War.

battles.loc: Seta Bridge, Ōmi Province (modern Ōtsu, Shiga)
battles.a: Prince Ōama's forces — Prince Ōama (later Emperor Tenmu)
battles.b: The Ōmi court — Emperor Kōbun (Prince Ōtomo)
battles.o: Decisive victory for Prince Ōama, who took the throne as Emperor Tenmu.

battles.loc: The Itabitsu River, near Dazaifu (modern Kitakyūshū)
battles.a: Imperial court forces — Ōno no Azumahito
battles.o: Rebellion crushed; Hirotsugu executed — but it shook Emperor Shōmu deeply.

battles.loc: Isawa, Mutsu Province — the Kitakami valley (modern Iwate)
battles.a: Imperial forces — Ki no Kosami (789), then Sakanoue no Tamuramaro
battles.o: After a famous early defeat, the court subdues the Emishi; Aterui surrenders in 802.

battles.loc: Mount Shigi, Kawachi/Yamato border (modern Nara/Osaka)
battles.a: Soga clan (pro-Buddhist) — Soga no Umako (with Prince Shōtoku)
battles.o: Soga victory; the Mononobe destroyed — securing Buddhism's place in the Japanese state.

battles.loc: Akasaka Castle, Kawachi Province (modern Chihaya-Akasaka, Osaka)
battles.o: Akasaka fell, but Kusunoki escaped by faking his death — keeping Go-Daigo's revolt alive.

battles.loc: Bubaigawara, Musashi Province (modern Fuchū, Tokyo)
battles.o: Nitta victory; the road to Kamakura lay open — and the shogunate fell days later.
